verses.text: And David consulted with the captains of thousands and hundreds, and with every leader. And David said unto all the congregation of Israel, If it seem good unto you, and that it be of the LORD our God, let us send abroad unto our brethren every where, that are left in all the land of Israel, and with them also to the priests and Levites which are in their cities and suburbs, that they may gather themselves unto us: And let us bring again the ark of our God to us: for we enquired not at it in the days of Saul. And all the congregation said that they would do so: for the thing was right in the eyes of all the people. Guidance: No sooner is David's kingdom established than his first great concern is for the ark of God — the symbol of the LORD's presence and throne among His people, neglected throughout Saul's reign. David rightly says, 'for we enquired not at it in the days of Saul,' marking the very fault that had ruined Saul (10:13-14) and resolving that his kingdom would be different — centered on God. He consults widely and seeks the people's glad agreement, and 'the thing was right in the eyes of all the people.' The chapter is showing us a king whose first priority is the worship and presence of God; before palaces or conquests, David wants the ark brought home. The chapter is teaching us the mark of a heart after God's own heart: to put the things of God first, to grieve over their neglect, and to long for the LORD's presence to be central among His people. The chapter is teaching us, too, the wisdom of seeking God's will ('that it be of the LORD our God') and of carrying the people along with us in good undertakings, rather than acting alone. David's desire was excellent, his consultation wise, his zeal genuine. Yet, as the chapter will soon show, even the best intentions must be governed by God's revealed word; right ends do not excuse wrong means. For now we are taught the loveliness of a leader who makes the presence of God his first business — would that every home and church set the worship and nearness of God at the center, lamenting where He has been neglected and longing to bring Him home.

So David gathered all Israel together, from Shihor of Egypt even unto the entering of Hemath, to bring the ark of God from Kirjathjearim. And David went up, and all Israel, to Baalah, that is, to Kirjathjearim, which belonged to Judah, to bring up thence the ark of God the LORD, that dwelleth between the cherubims, whose name is called on it. And they carried the ark of God in a new cart out of the house of Abinadab: and Uzza and Ahio drave the cart. And David and all Israel played before God with all their might, and with singing, and with harps, and with psalteries, and with timbrels, and with cymbals, and with trumpets. Guidance: All Israel gathers, from the river of Egypt to the entrance of Hamath, in a great festival of joy — singing and playing before God 'with all their might,' with harps, timbrels, cymbals, and trumpets. The zeal and gladness are wonderful. But hidden in the scene is a fatal error: 'they carried the ark of God in a new cart.' This was not the way God had commanded. The law required that the ark be carried on the shoulders of the Levites, by its staves, and never touched (Num 4:15; 7:9); the new cart was the method of the Philistines (1 Sam 6), borrowed from the pagans, not drawn from the word of God. The chapter is showing us sincere, exuberant worship undertaken in a way God had not appointed — great joy, great music, great crowds, and a great mistake at the heart of it. The chapter is teaching us that zeal without knowledge is dangerous; the loudest praise and the largest gathering cannot sanctify a method God has forbidden. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD must be served in the LORD's way — that worship is to be regulated by His word, not by what seems impressive or convenient, nor by copying the world's patterns. David and Israel meant well, played with all their might, and yet were about to grieve God by their carelessness about His commands. The chapter is teaching us to search the Scriptures for how God would be approached and worshiped, lest our very devotion go astray for want of obedience. Sincerity is necessary, but not sufficient; the new cart, however festive, was no substitute for the shoulders of the Levites that God had ordained.

And when they came unto the threshingfloor of Chidon, Uzza put forth his hand to hold the ark; for the oxen stumbled. And the anger of the LORD was kindled against Uzza, and he smote him, because he put his hand to the ark: and there he died before God. And David was displeased, because the LORD had made a breach upon Uzza: wherefore that place is called Perezuzza to this day. Guidance: The festival turns to horror. The oxen stumble, Uzza instinctively reaches out to steady the ark, and 'the anger of the LORD was kindled against Uzza, and he smote him... and there he died before God.' This severe judgment shocks us, as it shocked David, who was 'displeased' and named the place Perez-uzza, 'the breach of Uzza.' Why such severity for what seems a well-meant reflex? Because the whole undertaking had ignored God's express command: the ark was never to be touched by human hands, and had it been borne on the Levites' shoulders as the law required, no steadying hand would ever have been needed. Uzza's act, however instinctive, treated the holy ark as a common thing that human hands might prop up. The chapter is showing us the awful holiness of God, and that He will be sanctified before those who draw near to Him (cf. Lev 10:3). The chapter is teaching us that God's holiness is not to be presumed upon, that familiarity with sacred things can breed a carelessness that is itself an affront, and that the LORD sometimes makes a 'breach' to teach His people reverence the easy way had failed to teach. The chapter is teaching us, soberly, that good intentions do not nullify the requirements of God's word; the cart that should never have been used set the stage for the tragedy. The chapter is teaching us to fear God and to handle holy things with holy care. Yet it is also teaching us the mercy of the cross for us who live after it: the very holiness that struck Uzza is the holiness our great High Priest satisfied, so that we may now come boldly to the throne of grace — not by careless presumption, but through the blood of Christ, the only way God has appointed for sinners to draw near and live.

And David was afraid of God that day, saying, How shall I bring the ark of God home to me? So David brought not the ark home to himself to the city of David, but carried it aside into the house of Obededom the Gittite. And the ark of God remained with the family of Obededom in his house three months. And the LORD blessed the house of Obededom, and all that he had. Guidance: Shaken by the death of Uzza, David is 'afraid of God that day' and asks, 'How shall I bring the ark of God home to me?' Unsure how to proceed, he leaves the ark at the house of Obed-edom the Gittite — and there a beautiful thing happens: 'the LORD blessed the house of Obed-edom, and all that he had.' The very ark whose mishandling brought death to Uzza became a fountain of blessing to the household that received it rightly and reverently. The chapter is showing us the two faces of God's presence: a savor of death to those who treat Him carelessly, and a savor of life and blessing to those who receive Him in reverence and faith. The same ark, the same God — but Uzza died and Obed-edom was blessed. The chapter is teaching us that the presence of God is not to be feared by the humble and reverent but welcomed as the greatest of all blessings; where God dwells in truth, He blesses 'all that he had.' The chapter is teaching us that David's holy fear was right and good — better to pause and learn God's way than to rush on presumptuously; his question 'How shall I bring the ark?' was the beginning of wisdom, and it would lead him, three months later, to do it rightly (as chapter 15 will show, by the Levites and according to the word). The chapter is teaching us that a reverent fear of God is the path to recovered blessing, and that the household which gladly takes in the presence of God finds, not danger, but abundant grace. Obed-edom's blessing is the LORD's gentle assurance that He delights to dwell with His people and to enrich those who honour Him — and an encouragement to David, and to us, that the presence we rightly revere is the presence we may joyfully receive.
